coredata.dc:title: Galvanic replacement reaction-regulated photoelectric response and enzyme-mimicking property of ionic liquid functionalized Cu@Cu2O aerogels for dual-mode immunoassay
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jinli
Wang, Hengjia
Liu, Mingwang
Qin, Ying
Tan, Rong
Hu, Liuyong
Gu, Wenling
Zhu, Chengzhou
coredata.dc:description: Prostate-specific antigen (PSA) is a characteristic serum biomarker for screening prostate cancer, which benefits the prostate cancer diagnosis at an early stage. At present, the reported strategies for PSA detection are mainly simplex signal readout, inevitably limiting the inherent accuracy and reliability of the involved bioassay. In this work, ionic liquid functionalized Cu@Cu2O aerogels (IL-Cu@Cu2O) with the photoelectric response and enzyme-mimicking activity are used to construct a dual-mode biosensing platform toward PSA with Ag nanoparticles as the signaling labels and amplifiers. After the sandwich immunocomplexing, Ag+ ions could be subsequently released and directly interact with IL-Cu@Cu2O aerogels via galvanic replacement reaction, resulting in the enhanced photoelectrochemical (PEC) signal and decreased enzyme-mimicking activity for dual-mode signal readout of the PSA concentration. As a result, the proposed strategy presents wide detection ranges of 0.1 pg/mL to 5 ng/mL (PEC) and 5 pg/mL to 1 ng/mL (colorimetric) and ultralow limits of detection of 0.04 pg/mL (PEC) and 0.63 pg/mL (colorimetric) for PSA, which provides great prospects for highly accurate disease diagnosis in practical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-mode biosensing
Photoelectrochemical biosensing
Nanozymes
Colorimetric biosensing
Galvanic replacement reaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Immunoadsorption assay and PEC measurement toward PSA concentrations
Characterization of IL-AgCu@Cu2O aerogels and galvanic replacement reaction-regulated property of IL-Cu@Cu2O aerogels
PEC and peroxidase-like activities regulation mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM (inset: digital photograph), (b) TEM (inset: SAED pattern), and (c) HRTEM images of IL-Cu@Cu2O. HAADF-STEM (d) and HAADF-STEM mapping images of Cu (e), O (f) and their overlaps (g) of IL-Cu@Cu2O.
(a) SEM, (b) TEM (inset: SAED pattern) and (c) HRTEM images of IL-AgCu@Cu2O. HAADF-STEM (d) and HAADF-STEM mapping images of Cu (e), O (f), and Ag (g) of IL-AgCu@Cu2O.

(a) XRD patterns of IL-Cu@Cu2O and IL-AgCu@Cu2O aerogels. High-resolution XPS spectra of Cu 2p (b), O 1 s (c), and Ag 3d (d) orbits of IL-Cu@Cu2O and IL-AgCu@Cu2O aerogels. (e) Photocurrent responses of IL-Cu@Cu2O and IL-AgCu@Cu2O. (f) Peroxidase-like activities of IL-Cu@Cu2O and IL-AgCu@Cu2O.
Schematic band structures of IL-Cu@Cu2O and IL-AgCu@Cu2O.

(a) Photocurrents of IL-AgCu@Cu2O aerogel-based sensors at different concentrations of PSA and (b) the corresponding calibration curve and linear relation. (c) Selectivity of IL-Cu@Cu2O aerogel-based photoelectrodes for PSA detection. (d) UV–vis absorbance of IL-AgCu@Cu2O aerogel-based aerogels at different concentrations of PSA and (e) the corresponding calibration curve and linear relation. (f) Selectivity of IL-Cu@Cu2O aerogel for PSA detection.
Schematic illustration for the dual-mode biosensing platform of IL-Cu@Cu2O.

head.ce:title: Galvanic replacement reaction-regulated photoelectric response and enzyme-mimicking property of ionic liquid functionalized Cu@Cu2O aerogels for dual-mode immunoassay

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Pesticide and Chemical Biology of Ministry of Education, International Joint Research Center for Intelligent Biosensing Technology and Health, College of Chemistry, Central China Normal University, Wuhan 430079, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Plasma Chemistry and Advanced Materials
Hubei Engineering Technology Research Center of Optoelectronic and New Energy Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electronic and Information Engineering, Jingchu University of Technology, Jingmen 448000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electronic and Information Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electronic and Information Engineering, Jingchu University of Technology, Jingmen 448000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Prostate-specific antigen (PSA) is a characteristic serum biomarker for screening prostate cancer, which benefits the prostate cancer diagnosis at an early stage. At present, the reported strategies for PSA detection are mainly simplex signal readout, inevitably limiting the inherent accuracy and reliability of the involved bioassay. In this work, ionic liquid functionalized Cu@Cu2O aerogels (IL-Cu@Cu2O) with the photoelectric response and enzyme-mimicking activity are used to construct a dual-mode biosensing platform toward PSA with Ag nanoparticles as the signaling labels and amplifiers. After the sandwich immunocomplexing, Ag+ ions could be subsequently released and directly interact with IL-Cu@Cu2O aerogels via galvanic replacement reaction, resulting in the enhanced photoelectrochemical (PEC) signal and decreased enzyme-mimicking activity for dual-mode signal readout of the PSA concentration. As a result, the proposed strategy presents wide detection ranges of 0.1 pg/mL to 5 ng/mL (PEC) and 5 pg/mL to 1 ng/mL (colorimetric) and ultralow limits of detection of 0.04 pg/mL (PEC) and 0.63 pg/mL (colorimetric) for PSA, which provides great prospects for highly accurate disease diagnosis in practical application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Prostate cancer is the second most frequent new case of malignancy cancer among males worldwide [1–3]. To improve the survival rate of patients, it is crucial to diagnose prostate cancer at an early stage [4,5]. As a proverbial serum biomarker for screening prostate cancer [6–8], prostate-specific antigen (PSA) has become the most accurate and common biomarker for the early diagnosis of prostate cancer since its discovery [9]. Hitherto, different strategies have been developed to monitor the PSA levels in vitro or in vivo for early diagnosis, including surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [10,11], fluorescence [12,13], colorimetry [14,15], electrochemical method [16,17], and photoelectrochemical (PEC) assay [18–21]. Nonetheless, the known methods are mainly based on simplex signal readout, making it difficult for accurate and reliable detection due to the ultralow concentration of PSA in serum at an early stage [22]. In this regard, the dual-mode strategy provides great possibilities for PSA detection with high accuracy and persuasiveness [23], while most probes are single response for the target. Thus, developing multifunctional material with impressive dual-signal response becomes indispensable to build advanced biosensing platforms [24].

Metal-based aerogels (MAs), which are assemblies of nanomaterial in the form of three-dimensional (3D) self-supported macroscopic frameworks, have gained momentum in recent years [25–27]. Because of the attractive advantages, such as metallic building blocks, low densities, high porosities, and self-supportabilities [28–30], MAs have ignited a surge of unprecedented interest in material science and nanotechnology. Noteworthily, by finely regulating the composition and valence states of the metallic backbone, the catalytic performance and physicochemical properties of MAs can be adjusted accordingly, making MAs promising candidates with multiple signal responses for dual-mode biosensing. Thereinto, copper-based MAs exhibit the distinct merits of plasma effect, electrical conductivity, and earth abundance [31,32]. In addition, copper-contained compounds have demonstrated superb photoelectric properties for suitable bandgap [33] and prominent enzyme-like activity for the multiple electronic valence [34], which provide great prospects for the exploration of copper-based MAs as an attractive dual signal generator (photoelectrochemical and colorimetric signals). To achieve sensitive dual-mode biosensing, a rational design of chemical composition and interface structure of copper-based MAs with high photoelectric response and enzyme-mimicking property still remains challenging.

In this work, ionic liquid-functionalized Cu@Cu2O aerogels (IL-Cu@Cu2O) with both photoelectric response and enzyme-mimicking property were innovatively designed and prepared for dual-mode biosensing. The narrow bandgap and plasma effect of IL-Cu@Cu2O are beneficial for photon harvesting and charge separation, which is in favor of producing a strong PEC signal. In addition, IL-Cu@Cu2O was found with an efficient peroxidase-like activity, which can realize the oxidation reaction of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of H2O2 and cause the color changes. Therefore, the bifunctional IL-Cu@Cu2O exhibits great potential to construct an advanced dual-mode biosensing platform. By using Ag nanoparticles (NPs) as signaling labels and signal amplifiers, numerous Ag+ ions can be subsequently release to interact with IL-Cu@Cu2O via galvanic replacement reaction after the sandwich immunocomplexing, which resulted in enhanced PEC response and depressed peroxidase-like activity (Scheme 1 ). Finally, the dual-mode biosensing strategy based on PEC and colorimetric methods was employed for sensitive and accurate PSA detection, which not only sheds light on MAs for biosensing applications but gives new insights for further exploration of dual-mode immunoassay.

1 mL of IL (0.2 M), 5 mL of CuCl2 solution (0.2 M) and 5 mL of NaOH solution (0.4 M) were added to 300 mL of deionized water successively and stirred at room temperature for 20 min under N2 flow. Then, 300 μL of ethanol and 100 mg NaBH4 in 25 mL water were separately added to the mixture solution. After 40 min, the as-prepared hydrogels were washed and supercritical drying for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immunoadsorption assay and PEC measurement toward PSA concentrations
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of Ag NPs and the immunoadsorption assay were described in the supporting information. A droplet (10 µL) of the resultant IL-AgCu@Cu2O aerogel suspension was drop-cast on the indium tin oxide (ITO) slices and dried in an oven at 50 °C. The PEC measurement was performed on a CHI842d electrochemical workstation at −0.2 V bias with a classical three-electrode system using IL-AgCu@Cu2O modified ITO, Pt-wire, and Ag/AgCl as working electrode, auxiliary electrode, and reference electrode respectively and 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) as electrolyte solution. Xenon Light Source with a 420 nm cutoff filter was utilized as the light source.

The colorimetric test of PSA was confirmed by the color change of TMB before and after being oxidized by H2O2 under the catalysis of IL-AgCu@Cu2O. Specifically, 10 µL of the as-prepared IL-AgCu@Cu2O was added into the mixture of 100 µL H2O2 (0.1 M), 100 µL TMB (1 mM) and 100 µL acetic acid buffer (HAc-NaAc, 0.1 M, pH 4.0) in the 96-well plate. The mixture was mixed adequately on a shaker at 25 °C and the characteristic adsorption peak at 652 nm was recorded.

The IL-Cu@Cu2O aerogels were prepared by a simple wet-chemical method using NaBH4 as reducing agent [35]. To prove that the IL was absorbed in Cu@Cu2O aerogels surface, the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) was performed in Fig. S1 a. The peaks at 2922 and 1384 cm−1 are assigned to the CH asymmetric stretching of methylene group and CN vibration [36], indicating the successful modification of IL on Cu@Cu2O aerogels. The positively charged IL was functionalized on the surface of aerogels to promote the transfer of photo-induced electrons generated by the p-type Cu@Cu2O and stabilize the photocurrent (Fig. S1 b) by adjusting the surface charge polarity according to the zeta potential in Fig. S1 c. In addition, the amino group of IL can accelerate the gelation kinetics (less than 40 min) of MAs for its hydrophilicity and low interfacial tension. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image reveals the formation of porous nanostructure of IL-Cu@Cu2O (Fig. 1
a). The fluffy and porous structure with low density and high specific surface area can expose more accessible reactive sites and accelerate mass and charge transfer (inset in Fig. 1 a) [37]. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image further confirms the chain-like network morphology of IL-Cu@Cu2O (Fig. 1 b). The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (inset in Fig. 1 b) demonstrates the weak crystallinity of IL-Cu@Cu2O. As shown in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in Fig. 1 c, the lattice fringes with interplanar space of 0.242 and 0.209 nm are ascribed to the Cu2O (111) and Cu (111) facets of IL-Cu@Cu2O aerogels, respectively [38,39]. High-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) and corresponding energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (STEM-EDX) mapping images (Fig. 1 d-g) and EDX line scan profile (Fig. S2) indicate the uniform distribution of Cu and O elements within IL-Cu@Cu2O aerogels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of IL-AgCu@Cu2O aerogels and galvanic replacement reaction-regulated property of IL-Cu@Cu2O aerogels
ce:section.ce:para:
The doping of Ag was realized via galvanic replacement reaction to modulate the properties of IL-Cu@Cu2O aerogels, leading to the formation of IL-AgCu@Cu2O aerogels. As shown in Fig. 2
a and b, the as-prepared IL-AgCu@Cu2O aerogels show a similar chain-like structure with interplanar spaces of 0.250 and 0.209 nm, which are ascribed to Cu2O (111) and Cu (111) facets, respectively (Fig. 2 b inset and 2c) [40,41]. Due to the larger atomic radius of Ag, the lattice spaces of Cu2O (111) are magnified [42]. The HAADF-STEM mapping images in Fig. 2 d-g manifest the successful doping of Ag and the uniform distribution of Ag, Cu, and O throughout the nanowire.

To further confirm the crystal and electronic structure of IL-Cu@Cu2O and IL-AgCu@Cu2O, X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. S3 a, b) were employed. Five diffraction peaks can be indexed as the lattice of Cu (PDF No. 04-0836, blue line) and Cu2O (PDF No. 34-1354, green line) (Fig. 3
a) [43,44]. Partially magnified XRD patterns show that the peak of (111) plane of Cu2O shifts to a lower degree after the replacement of Cu+ with Ag+ (Fig. S4), implying a slight lattice expansion due to the larger atomic radius of Ag. Inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) data was collected to confirm the Ag content in IL-AgCu@Cu2O, and the ultra-low Ag percentage of 0.11 wt% explains the absence of Ag-related crystalline phase in the XRD pattern. The Cu contents reduced from 18.73 wt% in IL-Cu@Cu2O to 17.28 wt% in IL-AgCu@Cu2O after the galvanic replacement reaction (Table S1). High-resolution XPS spectrum of the Cu 2p shows two main peaks at 932.01 and 951.82 eV originating from Cu0/Cu+ (Fig. 3 b) [45–47]. By careful comparison, we found that the area percentage of Cu+ decreased from 88.26 % to 79.05 % after the galvanic replacement reaction. We conclude that the galvanic replacement reaction is taken place between Cu+ and Ag+. The Auger electron spectrum of Cu in Fig. S3 c shows a peak at 569.80 eV, which is in correspondence with Cu2O [45,48]. The O 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3 c) exhibits three peaks at 532.07, 531.32 and 530.03 eV, which originate from the adsorbed oxygen on the surface of aerogels, the oxygen vacancy in the Cu2O lattice defect and the oxygen element in Cu2O lattice, respectively [49,50]. The diverse oxygen species greatly benefit the catalytic activity of 3D IL-Cu@Cu2O aerogels. After the doping of Ag, the peak positions of O 1 s shift to higher energy, indicating the electronic interaction between Ag and Cu2O, leading to the different performance of IL-AgCu@Cu2O and IL-Cu@Cu2O. The remarkable decrease in oxygen vacancy will increase the crystal order and decrease the electron-hole recombination sites and undesired charge recombination, which may be in favor of photocurrent response [51]. As can be seen in Fig. 3 d, the typical peaks derived from Ag 3d orbits suggest the existence of metallic Ag in IL-AgCu@Cu2O as well [52]. The above results elucidate the successful doping of Ag and the potential interactions between Ag and Cu@Cu2O. To investigate the PEC and enzyme-mimicking performance of IL-Cu@Cu2O and IL-AgCu@Cu2O, the photocurrent and UV–vis absorbance of the chromogenic reaction product (oxTMB) were recorded. As expected, the photocurrent response of IL-AgCu@Cu2O is stronger than that of IL-Cu@Cu2O (Fig. 3 e), which presents a superior PEC performance of IL-AgCu@Cu2O to IL-Cu@Cu2O. Furthermore, the peroxidase-mimicking activity of IL-Cu@Cu2O is passivated after the galvanic replacement reaction (Fig. 3 f), which furnishes IL-Cu@Cu2O with a perspective in realizing dual-mode signal readout for a dual-mode biosensing platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEC and peroxidase-like activities regulation mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: To study the effect of Ag doping on the PEC performance, the band alignments of IL-Cu@Cu2O and IL-AgCu@Cu2O were investigated. The Mott-Schottky measurements and Tauc plots converted from UV–vis diffuse reflection spectrum of Cu2O were adopted (Fig. S5 a, b and c). The valence band (VB) and band gap (E g) are 1.24 V (vs Ag/AgCl, pH 7, unless otherwise specified) and 1.99 eV for Cu2O. The conduction band (CB) of Cu2O is calculated at −0.75 V. As the absolute electrode potential of the SCE vs vacuum is −4.4 V, the Fermi levels of Cu and Ag were calculated as 0.29 and −0.1 V vs Ag/AgCl [53,54]. The band structures of IL-Cu@Cu2O and IL-AgCu@Cu2O were shown in Fig. 4 . The introduction of a middle energy level platform of Ag facilitates the transfer of photo-induced electrons under irradiation, resulting in better electron-hole separation. Furthermore, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) results were recorded in Fig. S6, which presents a lower charge transfer resistance (R ct) of IL-AgCu@Cu2O than IL-Cu@Cu2O, explaining the better PEC response of IL-AgCu@Cu2O as well. In a typical peroxidase-like catalytic reaction, the redox couple is critical for the decomposition of H2O2 and the following catalytic kinetics [55–58]. Cu+, as an intermediate state of chemical valence, displays peroxidase-mimicking activity by decomposing H2O2 into reactive hydroxyl groups [59]. As demonstrated in the XPS results, the content of Cu+ is reduced in the aerogel frameworks after the galvanic replacement reaction, which leads to fewer Cu2+/Cu+ redox couples, and results in few catalytic sites toward the activation of H2O2.

Based on the adjustable PEC and peroxidase-like activity of IL-Cu@Cu2O via galvanic replacement reaction, the dual-mode immunoassay of PSA was performed with PEC and colorimetric methods. By using Ag NPs as signaling labels and amplifiers, numerous Ag+ ions can be subsequently released to interact with IL-Cu@Cu2O via galvanic replacement reaction after the sandwich immunocomplexing, which results in enhanced PEC response and depressed peroxidase-like activity. Firstly, the Ag NPs were prepared and characterized. The XRD pattern and SEM image in Fig. S7 a and b indicate the uniform Ag NPs with a diameter of about 20 nm. After the incubation process, the anti-PSA McAb for labeling was successfully labeled by Ag NPs (Fig. S7 c-e). After the recognition between antigen and antibody, Ag+ ions were released from Ag NPs after acid etching, which will react with the IL-Cu@Cu2O electrodes and trigger the signal variation accordingly. Since the photocurrent enhancement was induced by the Ag NPs-labeled sandwich immunocoplexes, the photocurrent responses could be recorded under optimized bias (-0.2 V) (Fig. S8) to reflect the PSA concentrations (Fig. 5
a). The linear calibration curve was plotted with a wide linear range from 0.1 pg/mL to 5 ng/mL with a correlation coefficient of 0.99 and a limit of detection (LOD, S/N = 3) of 0.04 pg/mL, demonstrating an excellent sensitivity (Fig. 5 b). Meanwhile, the characteristic UV–vis adsorption peaks of oxTMB under the catalysis of IL-AgCu@Cu2O with the presence of H2O2 were recorded at 652 nm (Fig. 5 d). The result reflects a lower catalytic ability with the increase of PSA concentration, which is in accordance with the above results. The calibration curve shows a linear range from 5 pg/mL to 1 ng/mL and a LOD of 0.63 pg/mL (Fig. 5 e). Both PEC and colorimetric methods present lower detection limit than many reported methods (Table S2) and good selectivity to the interferents (100 ng/mL) including carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), estrogen receptor (ER), progesterone receptor (PR), human epidermal growth factor receptor-2 (HER-2), bovine serum albumin (BSA) and glucose (Glu) at a ∼ 10-fold higher concentration than PSA (8 ng/mL) (Fig. 5 c, f). Moreover, the excellent stability of the IL-AgCu@Cu2O aerogel-based sensor is verified with a steady photocurrent in the presence of PSA (8 ng/mL) after 10 light “on–off” cycles (Fig. S9). At last, the dual-mode immunoassay was applied to the PSA detection in human serum (Table S3), which display high recoveries (93.75 to 100 %) and low relative standard deviations (RSD) (less than 5 %), indicating its high practicability and reliability.

In this work, IL-Cu@Cu2O aerogels with both remarkable PEC response and outstanding enzyme-like activity were prepared for Ag NPs-mediated dual-mode PSA immunoassay. After immunocomplexing, Ag+ ions released from the immobilized antibodies interact with IL-Cu@Cu2O aerogels via galvanic replacement reaction. As a result, the obvious changes in PEC response and enzyme-like activity of IL-Cu@Cu2O aerogels were observed, which were correlated to the PSA concentrations. Finally, the developed biosensing platform exhibits wide detection ranges, ultralow LOD and good selectivity with both PEC and colorimetric methods toward PSA. The photoactive and enzyme-mimicking IL-Cu@Cu2O aerogels realized a dual-mode detection strategy with dual signal readouts by a simple galvanic replacement reaction, which provides great prospects for dual-mode detection toward PSA and sheds light on multifunctional MAs for biosensing applications.

Jinli Li: Investigation, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Software, Writing – original draft. Hengjia Wang: Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Mingwang Liu: Writing – review & editing. Ying Qin: Methodology, Software, Visualization. Rong Tan: Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Liuyong Hu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Wenling Gu: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Chengzhou Zhu: Project administration, Supervision, Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22104114), Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (2021CFB518), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (CCNU22JC006) and the Program of Introducing Talents of Discipline to Universities of China (111 program, B17019).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.140743.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
